title: Expected value with empirical probabilities
description: Find expected value based on empirical probabilities.

assessment_items.question_content:
A fictitious study to determine the growth rate in the European Union randomly asked $5000$ adults in the EU how many children they would ideally have, given the options of $0$ children, $1$ child, or $2$ children. The results of this survey are shown below.

**Plot points to represent the expected sum of the ideal number of children for each number of randomly selected adults.**

Ideal number of children| Absolute frequency
:-: | :-: 
$0$ | $1200$
$1$ | $100$ 
$2$ | $3700$
Total | $5000$

[[☃ plotter 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$  $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Absolute frequency}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

The size of the sample space is $\red{\text{$5000$}}$. 

We need to find relative frequencies for each possible ideal number of children.

Ideal number of children| Absolute frequency | Relative frequency
:-: | :-: | :-:
$0$ | $1200$| $\dfrac{1200}{\red{5000}} = 0.24$ 
$1$ | $100$ | $\dfrac{100}{\red{5000}} = 0.02$
$2$ | $3700$| $\dfrac{3700}{\red{5000}} =0.74$
Total | $\red{5000}$| $1.00$ 

The sum of the values of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ is

$\qquad\left(0.24 \cdot 0\right) + \left(0.02 \cdot 1 \right) + \left(0.74 \cdot 2\right) = \pink{1.5}$ children.

We would expect $1$ randomly selected adult to want $1 \cdot \pink{1.5} = 1.5$ children.

We would expect $2$ randomly selected adults to want $2 \cdot \pink{1.5} = 3$ children.

We would expect $3$ randomly selected adults to want $3 \cdot \pink{1.5} = 4.5$ children.

We would expect $4$ randomly selected adults to want $4 \cdot \pink{1.5} = 6$ children.

We would expect $5$ randomly selected adults to want $5 \cdot \pink{1.5} = 7.5$ children.

Plotting these points gives:
[[☃ image 1]]

labels: Expected sum of ideal number of children

assessment_items.question_content:
Carmen collected data while her basketball team shot free throws. There are $10$ players on the team and each player shot $3$ free throws. How many players made a certain number of free throws is shown in the table below.	

**Plot points to represent the expected sum of the quantity of free throws for each number of randomly selected players from this team.**	

Free throws made| Players
:-: | :-: 
$0$ | $1$
$1$ | $5$ 
$2$ | $2$
$3$| $2$
Total | $10$

[[☃ plotter 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$  $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Absolute frequency}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

The size of the sample space is $\red{\text{$10$}}$. 

We need to find relative frequencies for each possible number of free throws made.

Free throws made| Players | Relative frequency
:-: | :-: | :-:
$0$ | $1$| $\dfrac{1}{\red{10}} = 0.1$ 
$1$ | $5$ | $\dfrac{5}{\red{10}} = 0.5$
$2$ | $2$| $\dfrac{2}{\red{10}} =0.2$
$3$ | $2$| $\dfrac{2}{\red{10}} =0.2$
Total | $\red{10}$| $1.00$ 

The sum of the values of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ is

$\qquad\left(0.1 \cdot 0\right) + \left(0.5 \cdot 1\right) + \left(0.2 \cdot 2\right) + \left(0.2 \cdot 3\right) = \pink{1.5}$ free throws made.

We would expect $1$ randomly selected player to make $1 \cdot \pink{1.5} = 1.5$ free throws.

We would expect $2$ randomly selected players to make $2 \cdot \pink{1.5} = 3$ free throws.

We would expect $3$ randomly selected players to make $3 \cdot \pink{1.5} = 4.5$ free throws.

We would expect $4$ randomly selected players to make $4 \cdot \pink{1.5} = 6$ free throws.

We would expect $5$ randomly selected players to make $5 \cdot \pink{1.5} = 7.5$ free throws.

Plotting these points gives:
[[☃ image 1]]

labels: Expected sum of free throws made

assessment_items.question_content:
Gordon sent out a survey to all of the customers that recently ate at his restaurant.  Each customer gave a ranking of the restaurant from $0$ to $3$ stars.  The number of customers that chose a certain number of stars is shown in the table below.

**Plot points to represent the expected sum of the number of stars for a randomly selected group of customers assuming that the survey results are representative for all of his customers.**

Stars| Customers
:-: | :-: 
$0$ | $10$
$1$ | $64$ 
$2$ | $42$
$3$ | $84$
Total | $200$
[[☃ plotter 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$  $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Absolute frequency}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

The size of the sample space is $\red{\text{$200$}}$. 

We need to find relative frequencies for each possible number of stars.

Stars| Customers | Relative frequency
:-: | :-: | :-:
$0$ | $10$| $\dfrac{10}{\red{200}} = 0.05$ 
$1$ | $64$ | $\dfrac{64}{\red{200}} = 0.32$
$2$ | $42$| $\dfrac{42}{\red{200}} =0.21$
$3$ | $84$| $\dfrac{84}{\red{200}} =0.42$
Total | $\red{200}$| $1.00$ 

The sum of the values of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ is

$\qquad\left(0.05 \cdot 0\right) + \left(0.32 \cdot 1\right) + \left(0.21 \cdot 2\right) + \left(0.42 \cdot 3\right) = \pink{2}$ stars.

We would expect $1$ randomly selected customer  to give $1 \cdot \pink{2} = 2$ stars.

We would expect $2$ randomly selected customers to give a total of $2 \cdot \pink{2} = 4$ stars.

We would expect $3$ randomly selected customers to give a total of $3 \cdot \pink{2} = 6$ stars.

We would expect $4$ randomly selected customers to give a total of $4 \cdot \pink{2} = 8$ stars.

We would expect $5$ randomly selected customers to give a total of $5 \cdot \pink{2} = 10$ stars.

Plotting these points gives:
[[☃ image 1]]

labels: Expected sum of number of stars

assessment_items.question_content:
Benjamin is the head coach of The Wildcats baseball team. Each weekend, The Wildcats play in a tournament that consists of $3$ games.

The Wildcats have already played in $20$ tournaments this season. Their results for those $20$ tournaments are shown in the table below. 

**Plot points to represent the expected sum of the quantity of wins after a certain number of tournaments assuming that The Wildcats continue to win at the same rate.**

Wins| Tournaments
:-: | :-: 
$0$ | $2$
$1$ | $3$ 
$2$ | $8$
$3$ | $7$
Total | $20$


[[☃ plotter 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$  $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Absolute frequency}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

The size of the sample space is $\red{\text{$20$}}$. 

We need to find relative frequencies for each possible number of wins.
Wins| Tournaments | Relative frequency
:-: | :-: | :-:
$0$ | $2$| $\dfrac{2}{\red{20}} = 0.1$ 
$1$ | $3$ | $\dfrac{3}{\red{20}} = 0.15$
$2$ | $8$| $\dfrac{8}{\red{20}} =0.4$
$3$ | $7$| $\dfrac{7}{\red{20}} =0.35$
Total | $\red{20}$| $1.00$ 

The sum of the values of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ is

$\qquad\left(0.1 \cdot 0\right) + \left(0.15 \cdot 1\right) + \left(0.4 \cdot 2\right) + \left(0.35 \cdot 3\right) = \pink{2}$ wins.

In $1$ tournament, we would expect The Wildcats to achieve $1 \cdot \pink{2} = 2$ wins.
In $2$ tournaments, we would expect The Wildcats to achieve $2 \cdot \pink{2} = 4$ wins.

In $3$ tournaments, we would expect The Wildcats to achieve $3 \cdot \pink{2} = 6$ wins.

In $4$ tournaments, we would expect The Wildcats to achieve $4 \cdot \pink{2} = 8$ wins.

In $5$ tournaments, we would expect The Wildcats to achieve $5 \cdot \pink{2} = 10$ wins.
Plotting these points gives:
[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Felipe opened up a unique bowling alley with lanes that have $2$ pins instead of the usual $10$. For each of his first $50$ guests, Felipe recorded how many pins they knocked down on their first bowl. The data he collected is shown in the table below.

**Plot points to represent the expected sum of the quantity of pins knocked down for each number of randomly selected guests.**

Pins knocked down| Guests
:-: | :-: 
$0$ | $20$
$1$ | $10$ 
$2$ | $20$
Total | $50$
[[☃ plotter 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$  $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Absolute frequency}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

The size of the sample space is $\red{\text{$50$}}$. 

We need to find relative frequencies for each possible number of pins knocked down.

Pins knocked down|Guests|Relative frequency
:-: | :-: | :-:
$0$ | $20$| $\dfrac{20}{\red{50}} = 0.4$ 
$1$ | $10$ | $\dfrac{10}{\red{50}} = 0.2$
$2$ | $20$| $\dfrac{20}{\red{50}} =0.4$
Total | $\red{50}$| $1.00$ 

The sum of the values of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ is

$\qquad\left(0.4 \cdot 0\right) + \left(0.2 \cdot 1 \right) + \left(0.4 \cdot 2\right) = \pink{1}$ pin knocked down.

We would expect $1$ randomly selected guest to knock down $1 \cdot \pink{1} = 1$ pin.

We would expect $2$ randomly selected guests to knock down $2 \cdot \pink{1} = 2$ pins.

We would expect $3$ randomly selected guests to knock down $3 \cdot \pink{1} = 3$ pins.

We would expect $4$ randomly selected guests to knock down $4 \cdot \pink{1} = 4$ pins.

We would expect $5$ randomly selected guests to knock down $5 \cdot \pink{1} = 5$ pins.

Plotting these points gives:
[[☃ image 1]]

labels: Expected sum of number of pins knocked down

assessment_items.question_content:
Sal is working on the customer service of the restaurant that he owns. He recently asked $100$ customers how many ice cubes they prefer in their drink. The results of this survey are shown in the table below.

**Plot points to represent the expected sum of the quantity of ice cubes for each number of randomly selected guests.**

Ice cubes| Guests
:-: | :-: 
$0$ | $50$
$1$ | $20$ 
$2$ | $10$
$3$ | $20$
Total | $100$

[[☃ plotter 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$  $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Absolute frequency}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

The size of the sample space is $\red{\text{$100$}}$. 

We need to find relative frequencies for each possible number of ice cubes.

Ice cubes| Guests | Relative frequency
:-: | :-: | :-:
$0$ | $50$| $\dfrac{50}{\red{100}} = 0.5$ 
$1$ | $20$ | $\dfrac{20}{\red{100}} = 0.2$
$2$ | $10$| $\dfrac{10}{\red{100}} =0.1$
$3$ | $20$| $\dfrac{20}{\red{100}} =0.2$
Total | $\red{100}$| $1.00$ 

The sum of the values of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ is

$\qquad\left(0.5 \cdot 0\right) + \left(0.2 \cdot 1\right) + \left(0.1 \cdot 2\right) + \left(0.2 \cdot 3\right) = \pink{1}$ ice cube.

We would expect $1$ randomly selected guest to prefer $1 \cdot \pink{1} = 1$ ice cube.

We would expect $2$ randomly selected guests to prefer $2 \cdot \pink{1} = 2$ ice cubes.

We would expect $3$ randomly selected guests to prefer $3 \cdot \pink{1} = 3$ ice cubes.

We would expect $4$ randomly selected guests to prefer $4 \cdot \pink{1} = 4$ ice cubes.

We would expect $5$ randomly selected guests to prefer $5 \cdot \pink{1} = 5$ ice cubes.

Plotting these points gives:
[[☃ image 1]]

labels: Expected sum of number of ice cubes

assessment_items.question_content:
Tony operates a local skate park where there are currently $50$ skaters performing tricks. For each skater, he keeps track of how many of their first $3$ tricks they land.  How many skaters completed a certain number of tricks is shown in the table below.

**Plot points to represent the expected sum of the quantity of tricks landed for each number of randomly selected skaters.**

Tricks landed| Skaters
:-: | :-: 
$0$ | $3$
$1$ | $2$ 
$2$ | $12$
$3$ | $33$
Total | $50$

[[☃ plotter 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$  $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Absolute frequency}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

The size of the sample space is $\red{\text{$50$}}$. 

We need to find relative frequencies for each possible number of tricks landed.

Tricks landed| Skaters | Relative frequency
:-: | :-: | :-:
$0$ | $3$| $\dfrac{3}{\red{50}} = 0.06$ 
$1$ | $2$ | $\dfrac{2}{\red{50}} = 0.04$
$2$ | $12$| $\dfrac{12}{\red{50}} =0.24$
$3$ | $33$| $\dfrac{33}{\red{50}} =0.66$
Total | $\red{50}$| $1.00$

The sum of the values of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ is

$\qquad\left(0.06 \cdot 0\right) + \left(0.04 \cdot 1\right) + \left(0.24 \cdot 2\right) + \left(0.66 \cdot 3\right) = \pink{2.5}$ tricks landed.

We would expect $1$ randomly selected skater to land $1 \cdot \pink{2.5} = 2.5$ tricks.

We would expect $2$ randomly selected skaters to land $2 \cdot \pink{2.5} = 5$ tricks.

We would expect $3$ randomly selected skaters to land $3 \cdot \pink{2.5} = 7.5$ tricks.

We would expect $4$ randomly selected skaters to land $4 \cdot \pink{2.5} = 10$ tricks.

Plotting these points gives:
[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
A fictitious study randomly asked $2000$ Canadian high school students how many candy bars they sold as part of a recent fundraiser.  The results of this study are shown in the table below.

**Plot points to represent the expected sum of the quantity of candy bars sold for each number of randomly selected students.**

Candy bars| Students
:-: | :-: 
$1$ | $533$
$2$ | $1101$ 
$3$ | $199$
$4$| $167$
Total | $2000$

[[☃ plotter 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$  $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Absolute frequency}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

The size of the sample space is $\red{\text{$2000$}}$. 

We need to find relative frequencies for each possible number of candy bars.

Candy bars| Students | Relative frequency
:-: | :-: | :-:
$1$ | $533$| $\dfrac{533}{\red{2000}} = 0.2665$ 
$2$ | $1101$ | $\dfrac{1101}{\red{2000}} = 0.5505$
$3$ | $199$| $\dfrac{199}{\red{2000}} =0.0995$
$4$ | $167$| $\dfrac{167}{\red{2000}} =0.0835$
Total | $\red{2000}$| $1.00$ 

The sum of the values of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ is

$\qquad\left(0.2655 \cdot 1\right) + \left(0.5505 \cdot 2\right) + \left(0.0995 \cdot 3\right) + \left(0.0835 \cdot 4\right) = \pink{2}$ candy bars.

We would expect $1$ randomly selected student to have sold $1 \cdot \pink{2} = 2$ candy bars.

We would expect $2$ randomly selected students to have sold a total of $2 \cdot \pink{2} = 4$ candy bars.

We would expect $3$ randomly selected students to have sold a total of $3 \cdot \pink{2} = 6$ candy bars.

We would expect $4$ randomly selected students to have sold a total of $4 \cdot \pink{2} = 8$ candy bars.

We would expect $5$ randomly selected students to have sold a total of $5 \cdot \pink{2} = 10$ candy bars.

Plotting these points gives:
[[☃ image 1]]

labels: Expected sum of number of candy bars

assessment_items.question_content:
Rohit is an avid runner and has kept track of the distances of each of his last $50$ runs. The data he has collected on the distances of his runs are shown in the table below.

Each time Rohit goes running in the future, he is going to randomly select $1$ of these $50$ runs and repeat it.

**Plot points to represent the expected total number of miles ran for each number of randomly selected runs.**

Distance (miles)| Runs
:-: | :-: 
$1$ | $6$
$2$ | $13$ 
$3$ | $31$
Total | $50$

[[☃ plotter 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$  $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Absolute frequency}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

The size of the sample space is $\red{\text{$50$}}$. 

We need to find relative frequencies for each possible number of miles.

Miles| Runs | Relative frequency
:-: | :-: | :-:
$1$ | $6$ | $\dfrac{6}{\red{50}} = 0.12$
$2$ | $13$| $\dfrac{13}{\red{50}} =0.26$
$3$ | $31$| $\dfrac{31}{\red{50}} =0.62$
Total | $\red{50}$| $1.00$ 

The sum of the values of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ is

$\qquad\left(0.12 \cdot 1\right) + \left(0.26 \cdot 2\right) + \left(0.62 \cdot 3\right) = \pink{2.5}$ miles.

We would expect $1$ randomly selected run to be $1 \cdot \pink{2.5} = 2.5$ miles.

We would expect $2$ randomly selected runs to be a total of $2 \cdot \pink{2.5} = 5$ miles.

We would expect $3$ randomly selected runs to be a total of $3 \cdot \pink{2.5} = 7.5$ miles.

We would expect $4$ randomly selected runs to be a total of $4 \cdot \pink{2.5} = 10$ miles.

Plotting these points gives:
[[☃ image 1]]

labels: Expected total number of miles ran

assessment_items.question_content:
Hector is going to begin painting pictures and can’t decide how big he should make each painting. To help him decide, he put a survey up on his favorite social media site. The results of this survey are shown in the table below.

**Plot points to represent the sum of the area of a certain number of pictures if Hector chooses to make each painting the average survey response.**

Area of picture $(\text{in}^2)$| Survey responders
:-: | :-: 
$10$ | $3$
$20$ | $5$ 
$30$ | $11$
$40$|$11$
Total | $30$

[[☃ plotter 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$  $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Absolute frequency}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

The size of the sample space is $\red{\text{$30$}}$. 

We need to find relative frequencies for each possible area of a picture.

Area of picture $(\text{in}^2)$ | Survey responders | Relative frequency
:-: | :-: | :-:
$10$ | $3$| $\dfrac{3}{\red{30}}$ 
$20$ | $5$ | $\dfrac{5}{\red{30}}$
$30$ | $11$| $\dfrac{11}{\red{30}}$
$40$ | $11$| $\dfrac{11}{\red{30}}$
Total | $\red{30}$| $1.00$ 

The sum of the values of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ is

$\qquad\left(\dfrac{3}{\red{30}} \cdot 10\right) + \left(\dfrac{5}{\red{30}} \cdot 20\right) + \left(\dfrac{11}{\red{30}} \cdot 30\right) + \left(\dfrac{11}{\red{30}} \cdot 40\right) = \pink{30}$ $\text{in}^2$.

$1$ picture would have an area of $1 \cdot \pink{30} = 30$ $\text{in}^2$.

$2$ pictures would have an area of $2 \cdot \pink{30} = 60$ $\text{in}^2$.

$3$ pictures would have an area of $3 \cdot \pink{30} = 90$ $\text{in}^2$.

$4$ pictures would have an area of $4 \cdot \pink{30} = 120$ $\text{in}^2$.

$5$ pictures would have an area of $5 \cdot \pink{30} = 150$ $\text{in}^2$.

Plotting these points gives:
[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Benji is working to get a job but isn't sure how much he will be able to make. He visited a job search website with thousands of listings and documented a random selection of $50$ jobs at $5$ different starting salary levels. The data he recorded is in the table below.

**Assuming that jobs continue to be listed in the same starting salary proportions, which of the following statements are true?**

Starting salary| Jobs listed
:-: | :-: 
$\$30{,}000$ | $19$ 
$\$40{,}000$ | $2$ 
$\$50{,}000$ | $6$
$\$60{,}000$ | $17$
$\$70{,}000$ | $6$
Total | $50$



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$  $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Absolute frequency}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

The size of the sample space is $\red{\text{$50$}}$. 

We need to find relative frequencies for each possible salary.

The sum of the values of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ is:


$\left(0.38 \cdot \$30{,}000\right) + \left(0.04 \cdot \$40{,}000\right) + \left(0.12 \cdot \$50{,}000\right) + \left(0.34 \cdot \$60{,}000\right) + \left(0.12 \cdot \$70{,}000\right) = \$47{,}800$

The expected starting salary of a job on this job board is $\$47{,}800$, not $\$30{,}000$.
The expected value of the sum of $20$ randomly selected jobs on this job board is $20 \cdot \$47{,}800 = \$956{,}000$, not $\$600{,}000$.

Expected value is the value we expect *on average*, not the most likely salary we would expect for a randomly selected job. 

Since $\$30{,}000$ was the most common starting salary in the data, the most likely starting salary of a randomly selected job off of this job board is $\$30{,}000$.

We are expecting job salaries to continue to be listed in the same proportions.

Of the $\red{50}$ jobs,  $6$ of them have a starting salary of $\$50{,}000$ and $6$ of them have a starting salary of $\$70{,}000$.

If Benji were randomly selecting jobs off of this job board, in the long run he should expect to find the same number of jobs with a starting salary of $\$50{,}000$ as jobs with a starting salary of $\$70{,}000$.

The $2$ true statements are:

-  The most likely starting salary of a randomly selected job off of this job board is $\$30{,}000$.
- If Benji were randomly selecting jobs off of this job board, in the long run he should expect to find the same number of jobs with a starting salary of $\$50{,}000$ as jobs with a starting salary of $\$70{,}000$.

choices.content: The expected starting salary of a job on this job board is $\$30{,}000$.
If Benji were to randomly select an additional $20$ jobs off of this job board, the expected value of the sum of the starting salaries would be $\$600{,}000$.
The most likely starting salary of a randomly selected job off of this job board is $\$30{,}000$.

If Benji were randomly selecting jobs off of this job board, in the long run he should expect to find the same number of jobs with a starting salary of $\$50{,}000$ as jobs with a starting salary of $\$70{,}000$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Erin has been watching a lot of television lately. Each day for the last $100$ days, she has documented how many episodes of television she has watched. The data she recorded is in the table below.

**Assuming that Erin continues to watch television in the same proportions, which of the following statements are true?**

Number of episodes| Days
:-: | :-: 
$0$ | $7$ 
$1$ | $17$ 
$2$ | $6$
$3$ | $43$
$4$ | $16$
$5$ | $11$
Total | $100$



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$  $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Absolute frequency}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

The size of the sample space is $\red{\text{$100$}}$. 

We need to find relative frequencies for each possible number of episodes.

The sum of the values of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ is:


$\left(0.07 \cdot 0\right) + \left(0.17 \cdot 1\right) + \left(0.06 \cdot 2\right) + \left(0.43 \cdot 3\right) + \left(0.16 \cdot 4\right) + \left(0.11 \cdot 5\right) = 2.77$

The expected number of episodes that Erin watches in a day is $2.77$.
The expected total number of episodes that Erin will watch in the next $10$ days $10 \cdot 2.77 = 27.7$.

Expected value is the value we expect *on average*, not the most likely number of shows in a day.

 Since $3$ shows was the most common number of shows in Erin's data, the most likely number of shows in a day is $3$.

We are expecting Erin to watch television shows in the same proportions as in her data.

Of the $\red{100}$ days Erin collected data for, she watched $3$ shows on $43$ days and $1$ show on $17$ days.

We can expect Erin to watch $3$ shows $\dfrac{43}{17} \approx 2.53$ times as often as she watches $1$ show.

In the long run, we can expect Erin to watch $3$ episodes in a day more than twice as often as she watches $1$ episode in a day.

The $4$ true statements are:

-  The expected number of episodes that Erin watches in a day is $2.77$.
- During the next $10$ days, we would expect Erin to watch a total of $27.7$ episodes.
- The most likely number of episodes Erin will watch in a day is $3$.
- In the long run, we can expect Erin to watch $3$ episodes in a day more than twice as often as she watches $1$ episode in a day.

choices.content: The expected number of episodes that Erin watches in a day is $2.77$.
During the next $10$ days, we would expect Erin to watch a total of $27.7$ episodes.
The most likely number of episodes Erin will watch in a day is $3$.
In the long run, we can expect Erin to watch $3$ episodes in a day more than twice as often as she watches $1$ episode in a day.

assessment_items.question_content:
Sam is a salesman. The past $400$ days he has kept track of how many new clients he obtains each day. The data he recorded are in the table below.

**Assuming that he continues to obtain clients in the same proportions, which of the following statements are true?**

Number of new clients| Days
:-: | :-: 
$1$ | $153$ 
$2$ | $110$ 
$3$ | $77$
$4$ | $39$
$5$ | $21$
Total | $400$



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$  $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Absolute frequency}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

The size of the sample space is $\red{\text{$400$}}$. 

We need to find relative frequencies for each possible number of new clients.

The sum of the values of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ is:


$\left(0.3825 \cdot 1\right) + \left(0.275 \cdot 2\right) + \left(0.1925 \cdot 3\right) + \left(0.0975 \cdot 4\right) + \left(0.0525 \cdot 5\right) = 2.1625$

The expected value of new clients that Sam obtains in $1$ day rounded to the nearest hundredth is $2.16$.
The expected value of number of new clients over the next $5$ days is $5 \cdot 2.1625 \approx 10.81$.

Expected value is the value we expect *on average*, not the most likely number of clients in a day.

Since $1$ new client is the most common number of new clients in a day while Sam collected data, he is most likely to obtain $1$ new client the next day that he works.

We are expecting Sam to obtain new clients in the same proportions as in the data he collected. 

In the past $\red{400}$ days, he obtained $2$ new clients on $110$ days, and he obtained $5$ new clients on $21$ days. 

 We expect Sam to gain $2$ new clients $\dfrac{110}{21} \approx 5.24$ times as often as he gains  $5$ new clients. 

In the long run, Sam can expect to have days where he obtains $2$ new clients more than $5$ times as often as he has days where he obtains $5$ new clients.

The $2$ true statements are:

-  The expected value of new clients that Sam obtains in $1$ day rounded to the nearest hundredth is $2.16$.
- In the long run, Sam can expect to have days where he obtains $2$ new clients more than $5$ times as often as he has days where he obtains $5$ new clients.

choices.content: In the long run, Sam can expect to have days where he obtains $2$ new clients more than $5$ times as often as he has days where he obtains $5$ new clients.
Sam is most likely to obtain $2$ new clients the next day that he works.
Over the next $5$ days that Sam works, he can expect to obtain a total of $12$ new clients
The expected value of new clients that Sam obtains in $1$ day rounded to the nearest hundredth is $2.16$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Olga’s dad gave her a die for her birthday. She wanted to make sure it was a fair die so she conducted an experiment where she rolled it $500$ times and kept track of how many times the die rolled each number. The data she recorded is in the table below.

**Assuming that the die continues to roll in the same proportions, which of the following statements are true?**

Die value| Absolute frequency
:-: | :-: 
$1$ | $90$ 
$2$ | $110$ 
$3$ | $95$
$4$ | $70$
$5$ | $75$
$6$ | $60$
Total | $500$



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$  $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Absolute frequency}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

The size of the sample space is $\red{\text{$500$}}$. 

We need to find relative frequencies for each die value.

The sum of the values of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ is:


$\left(0.18 \cdot 1\right) + \left(0.22 \cdot 2\right) + \left(0.19 \cdot 3\right) + \left(0.14 \cdot 4\right)
 + \left(0.15 \cdot 5\right) + \left(0.12 \cdot 6\right) = 3.22$

The expected value per die roll is $3.22$, not $2$.
The expected value of Olga's next $20$ die rolls is $20 \cdot 3.22 = 64.4$.

Expected value is the value we expect *on average*, not the most likely value for a single roll. 

Since $2$ was the most common result in Olga’s experiment, she is most likely to roll a $2$ on her next roll.

We expect this die to roll in the same proportions as in her experiment.

 Of her $\red{500}$ rolls,  $110$ times she rolled a $2$ and $60$ times she rolled a $6$. 

We expect a roll of $2$ to occur $\dfrac{110}{60} = 1.83$ times as often as a roll of $6$. 

A $2$ is less than twice as common as a $6$.

The $2$ true statements are:

-  If Olga were to roll this die $20$ times the expected value of the sum of her die rolls would be $64.4$.
- The most likely outcome of her next die roll is $2$.

choices.content: The expected value of a single die roll is $2$.
If Olga were to roll this die $20$ times the expected value of the sum of her die rolls would be $64.4$.
The most likely outcome of her next die roll is $2$.
In the long run, Olga would expect a value of $2$ more than twice as often as a $6$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Tanner is an Ultimate Frisbee player.  He has made $100$ throws and recorded how many meters they traveled rounded to the nearest $10$ meters. The data he recorded is in the table below.

**Assuming the distance Tanner throws the disc continues in the same proportions, which of the following statements are true?**

Distance (meters)| Throws
:-: | :-: 
$10$ | $4$ 
$20$ | $21$ 
$30$ | $32$
$40$ | $35$
$50$ | $8$
Total | $100$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency}\cdot\text{value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Absolute frequency}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

The size of the sample space is $\redE{100}$.

We need to find relative frequencies for each possible distance.

The sum of the values of $\text{relative frequency}\cdot\text{value}$ is:

$\left(0.04 \cdot 10\right) + \left(0.21 \cdot 20\right) + \left(0.32 \cdot 30\right) + \left(0.35 \cdot 40\right) + \left(0.08 \cdot 50\right) = 32.2$

The expected distance of Tanner's next throw is $32.2$ meters.
The expected total distance of Tanner's next $50$ throws is $50 \cdot 32.2 = 1610$ meters.

Expected value is the value we expect *on average*, not the most likely distance for a single throw.

 Since $40$ meters was the most common distance Tanner threw the disc, his next throw is most likely to travel $40$ meters.

We expect Tanner to continue to throw the disc in the same proportions.

Of his $\redE{100}$ throws,  he threw the disc $40$ meters $35$ times and he threw the disc $50$ meters $8$ times. 

We expect Tanner to throw the disc $40$ meters $\dfrac{35}{8} = 4.375$ times as often as he throws the disc $50$ meters. 

In the long run, Tanner throwing the disc $40$ meters is more than $4$ times as likely as him throwing the disc $50$ meters.

The $2$ true statements are:

-  Tanner's next disc throw is expected to travel $32.2$ meters.
- In the long run, Tanner will throw the disc $40$ meters more than $4$ times as often as he throws the disc $50$ meters.

choices.content: In the long run, Tanner will throw the disc $40$ meters more than $4$ times as often as he throws the disc $50$ meters.
Tanner's next disc throw is expected to travel $32.2$ meters.
Tanner's next disc throw is most likely to travel $30$ meters.
The expected value of Tanner's next $50$ disc throws is $2000$ meters.

assessment_items.question_content:
Marc is a pharmacist and has documented the number of pills for the last $20$ prescriptions he has filled. The data he recorded is in the table below.

**Assuming that Marc continues to fill prescriptions with number of pills that follow the same proportions, which of the following statements are true?**

Number of pills| Prescriptions
:-: | :-: 
$10$ | $4$ 
$20$ | $4$ 
$30$ | $4$
$40$ | $5$
$50$ | $3$
Total | $20$



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$  $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Absolute frequency}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

The size of the sample space is $\red{\text{$20$}}$. 

We need to find relative frequencies for each possible number of pills.

The sum of the values of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ is:


$\left(0.2 \cdot 10\right) + \left(0.2 \cdot 20\right) + \left(0.2 \cdot 30\right) + \left(0.25 \cdot 40\right) + \left(0.15 \cdot 50\right) = 29.5$

The expected number of pills in the next prescription that Marc fills is $29.5$.
The expected value of the number of pills in the next $20$ prescriptions that Marc fills is $20 \cdot 29.5 = 590$.

Expected value is the value we expect *on average*, not the most likely number of pills in a prescription.

Since $40$ was the most common number of pills in a prescription that Marc filled, he is most likely to fill a prescription with $40$ pills.

We are expecting Marc to fill prescriptions with the number of pills in the same proportion as in the last $\red{20}$ prescriptions. 

Of his $\red{20}$ prescriptions,  $5$ prescriptions contained $40$ pills while $3$ prescriptions contained $50$ pills.  

In the long run, Marc can expect to fill prescriptions with $40$ pills more often than he fills prescriptions with $50$ pills.

The $3$ true statements are:

-  The expected number of pills in the next prescription that Marc fills is $29.5$.
- The most likely number of pills in the next prescription that Marc fills is $40$.
- In the long run, Marc can expect to fill prescriptions with $40$ pills more often than he fills prescriptions with $50$ pills.

choices.content: The expected number of pills in the next prescription that Marc fills is $29.5$.
The most likely number of pills in the next prescription that Marc fills is $40$.
The expected total number of pills in the next $20$ prescriptions that Marc fills is $680$.
In the long run, Marc can expect to fill prescriptions with $40$ pills more often than he fills prescriptions with $50$ pills.

assessment_items.question_content:
Tintin is studying alien bugs on Mars.  In her studies, Tintin has documented how many legs $200$ different bugs have. The data she recorded is in the table below.

**Assuming that the number of legs on a bug continues to be found in the same proportions, which of the following statements are true?**

Number of legs| Bugs
:-: | :-: 
$2$ | $43$ 
$4$ | $22$ 
$6$ | $30$
$8$ | $69$
$10$ | $36$
Total | $200$



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$  $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Absolute frequency}}{{\text{Size of the sample space}}}$ 

The size of the sample space is $\red{\text{$200$}}$. 

We need to find relative frequencies for each possible number of legs.

The sum of the values of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ is:

$\left(0.215 \cdot 2\right) + \left(0.11 \cdot 4\right) + \left(0.15 \cdot 6\right) + \left(0.345 \cdot 8\right) + \left(0.18 \cdot 10\right) = 6.33$

The expected number of legs per bug that Tintin finds is $6.33$, not $6.48$.
The expected total number of legs for the next $30$ bugs that Tintin finds is $30 \cdot  6.33 = 189.9$.
Since bugs with $4$ legs were the least common in Tintin's data, the next bug Tintin finds is least likely to have $4$ legs.

We are expecting bugs to be found with numbers of legs in the same proportions as in Tintin's data. 

Of the $\red{200}$ bugs Tintin found,  $43$ bugs had $2$ legs and $22$ bugs had $4$ legs.

We expect Tintin to find bugs with $2$ legs $\dfrac{43}{22} \approx 1.95$ times as often as she finds bugs with $4$ legs. 

In the long run, we can expect Tintin to find bugs with $2$ legs less than twice as often (not more than) as she finds bugs with $4$ legs.

The $2$ true statements are:

-  The expected total number of legs for the next $30$ bugs that Tintin finds is $189.9$.
- The next bug Tintin finds is least likely to have $4$ legs.

choices.content: The next bug Tintin finds is least likely to have $4$ legs.
The expected number of legs for the next bug that Tintin finds is $6.48$.
The expected total number of legs for the next $30$ bugs that Tintin finds is $189.9$.
In the long run, Tintin will find bugs with $2$ legs more than twice as often as she finds bugs with $4$ legs.

assessment_items.question_content:
Rohit is recapping his track season where he ran in a total of $24$ races. 

His coach tells him that $4$ of his races were $100$ meters in length and $2$ races were $400$ meters in length. He remembers that during the last $12$ races of the season, he was expected to run a total of $4200$ meters.  He’s also willing to assume that the lengths of these $12$ races are representative of the rest of the races.

He isn’t sure how many of the other races were $200$ meters long and how many were $800$ meters long. 

**Find the $2$ missing numbers from the data table below.**
 
Race length (meters)| Races
:-: | :-: 
$100$ | $4$
$200$ | [[☃ input-number 1]] 
$400$ | $2$
$800$ | [[☃ input-number 2]]
Total | $24$
hints: Since Rohit would expect to run a total of $4200$ meters over $12$ races, the expected number of meters per race is $\dfrac{4200}{12} = 350$.
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together. We need to find the relative frequencies.

Race length (meters)| Races| Relative frequency | Relative frequency $\cdot$ race length
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$100$ | $4$|$\dfrac{4}{\red{24}} = 0.1667$| $16.67$
$200$ | $\pink{A}$ | $\dfrac{\pink{A}}{\red{24}}$  | $\dfrac{200\pink{A}}{\red{24}}$
$400$ | $2$|$\dfrac{2}{\red{24}} = 0.0833$| $33.33$
$800$ | $\purple{B}$| $\dfrac{\purple{B}}{\red{24}}$| $\dfrac{800\purple{B}}{\red{24}}$
Total | $\red{24}$| $1.00$| $350$

Note that the sum of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ race length}$ is expected value, which we know to be $350$.

We can now create an equation based on expected value:

$\qquad$ $\begin{align} 16.67 + \dfrac{200\pink{A}}{\red{24}} + 33.33 + \dfrac{800\purple{B}}{\red{24}} &= 350 \\\\\\
200\pink{A} + 800\purple{B} &= 7200  \end{align}$

We can also create an equation based on absolute frequencies:

$\qquad$ $\begin{align} 4 + \pink{A} + 2 + \purple{B} &= \red{24} \\\\\\
\purple{B} &= 18 - \pink{A}
\end{align}$

Combining the $2$ equations and solving gives:

$\qquad$ $\begin{align}\pink{A} &= 12 \\\\
   \purple{B} &= 6\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Chef Jeff’s sale register system has broken down.  He needs to recover his sales data from the day. 
 
He recalls that he has filled a total of $100$ orders and $20$ of them have been $6$ piece chicken tender meals. He also knows that he has sold a total of $610$ chicken tender pieces today. 

He isn’t sure how many of the other orders were $3$ piece chicken tender meals and how many were $8$ piece chicken tender meals.  

**Find the $2$ missing numbers from the data table below.**
 
Chicken tender pieces| Orders
:-: | :-: 
$3$ | [[☃ input-number 1]]
$6$ | $20$ 
$8$ | [[☃ input-number 2]]
Total | $100$
hints: Since Chef Jeff has sold $610$ chicken tender pieces in $100$ orders, the expected number of chicken tender pieces per order is $\dfrac{610}{100} = 6.1$.
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together. We need to find the relative frequencies.

Chicken tender pieces| Orders| Relative frequency | Relative frequency $\cdot$ chicken tender pieces
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$3$ | $\pink{A}$ | $\dfrac{\pink{A}}{\red{100}}$  | $\dfrac{3\pink{A}}{\red{100}}$
$6$ | $20$|$\dfrac{20}{\red{100}} = 0.2$| $1.2$
$8$ | $\purple{B}$| $\dfrac{\purple{B}}{\red{100}}$| $\dfrac{8\purple{B}}{\red{100}}$
Total | $\red{100}$| $1.00$| $6.1$


Note that the sum of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ chicken tender pieces}$ is expected value, which we know to be $6.1$.

We can now create an equation based on expected value:

$\qquad$ $\begin{align} \dfrac{3\pink{A}}{\red{100}} + 1.2 +\dfrac{8\purple{B}}{\red{100}} &= 6.1 \\\\\\
3\pink{A} + 8\purple{B} &= 490  \end{align}$

We can also create an equation based on absolute frequencies:

$\qquad$ $\begin{align}\pink{A} + 20 + \purple{B} &= \red{100} \\\\\\
\purple{B} &= 80 - \pink{A}
\end{align}$

Combining the $2$ equations and solving gives:

$\qquad$ $\begin{align}\pink{A} &= 30 \\\\
   \purple{B} &= 50\end{align}$

Chicken tender pieces| Orders
:-: | :-: 
$3$ | $\pink{30}$
$6$ | $20$ 
$8$ | $\purple{50}$
Total | $\red{100}$

assessment_items.question_content:
In the last week, Daniela has played $10$ tennis matches. She remembers that her opponent had won $1$ set in $4$ of the matches and that her opponent was expected to win a total of $6$ sets over the course of $5$ matches.

Unfortunately, she can’t remember how many times her opponent won $0$ sets or $2$ sets. 

**Find the $2$ missing numbers from the data table below.**  
Assume Daniela's opponent won the expected number of sets over the course of the $10$ matches.
 
Sets won by opponent| Matches
:-: | :-: 
$0$ | [[☃ input-number 1]]
$1$ | $4$ 
$2$ | [[☃ input-number 2]]
Total | $10$
hints: Since Daniela's opponent is expected to win $6$ sets over $5$ matches, the expected number of sets per match is  $\dfrac{6}{5} = 1.2$.
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together. We need to find the relative frequencies.

Sets won by opponent| Matches| Relative frequency | Relative frequency $\cdot$ sets won by opponent
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$0$ | $\pink{A}$ | $\dfrac{\pink{A}}{\red{10}}$  | $0$
$1$ | $4$|$\dfrac{4}{\red{10}} = 0.4$| $0.4$
$2$ | $\purple{B}$| $\dfrac{\purple{B}}{\red{10}}$| $\dfrac{2\purple{B}}{\red{10}}$
Total | $\red{10}$| $1.00$| $1.2$


Note that the sum of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ sets won by opponent}$ is expected value, which we know to be $1.2$.

We can now create an equation based on expected value:

$\qquad$ $\begin{align} 0 + 0.4 + \dfrac{2\purple{B}}{\red{10}} &= 1.2 \\\\\\
\purple{B} &= 4  \end{align}$

We can also create an equation based on absolute frequencies:

$\qquad$ $\begin{align}\pink{A} + 4 + \purple{B} &= \red{10} \\\\\\
\end{align}$

Combining the $2$ equations and solving gives:

$\qquad$ $\begin{align}\pink{A} &= 2 \\\\
   \purple{B} &= 4\end{align}$

Sets won by opponent| Matches
:-: | :-: 
$0$ | $\pink{2}$
$1$ | $4$ 
$2$ | $\purple{4}$
Total | $\red{10}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Jamie's dad gave her a die for her birthday. She wanted to make sure it was fair so she took her die to school and rolled it $500$ times and kept track of how many times the die rolled each number. Afterwards she calculated  the expected value of the sum of $20$ rolls to be $67.4$. 

On her way home from school it was raining and $2$ values were washed away from her data table.

**Find the $2$ missing absolute frequencies from Jamie's data table.**
 
Die value| Absolute frequency
:-: | :-: 
$1$ | [[☃ input-number 1]]
$2$ | $110$ 
$3$ | $95$
$4$ | $70$
$5$ | $75$
$6$ | [[☃ input-number 2]]
Total | $500$
hints: Since the expected value of the sum of $20$ rolls is $67.4$, the expected value of $1$ roll must be $\dfrac{67.4}{20} = 3.37$.
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together. We need to find the relative frequencies.

Die value| Absolute frequency | Relative frequency | Relative frequency $\cdot$ die value
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$1$ | $\pink{A}$ | $\dfrac{\pink{A}}{\red{500}}$  | $\dfrac{\pink{A}}{\red{500}}$
$2$ | $110$|$\dfrac{110}{\red{500}} = 0.22$| $0.44$
$3$ | $95$ | $\dfrac{95}{\red{500}} = 0.19$| $0.57$
$4$ | $70$ |$\dfrac{ 70}{\red{500}} = 0.14$| $0.56$
$5$ | $75$ | $\dfrac{75}{\red{500}} = 0.15$| $0.75$
$6$ | $\purple{B}$| $\dfrac{\purple{B}}{\red{500}}$| $\dfrac{6\purple{B}}{\red{500}}$
Total | $\red{500}$| $1.00$| $3.37$


Note that the sum of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ die value}$ is expected value, which we know to be $3.37$.

We can now create an equation based on expected value:

$\qquad$ $\begin{align} \dfrac{\pink{A}}{\red{500}} + 0.44 +0.57 +0.56 + 0.75 + \dfrac{6\purple{B}}{\red{500}} &= 3.37 \\\\\\
\pink{A} + 6\purple{B} &= 525  \end{align}$

We can also create an equation based on absolute frequencies:

$\qquad$ $\begin{align}\pink{A} + 110 + 95 + 70 + 75 + \purple{B} &= \red{500} \\\\\\
\purple{B} &= 150 - \pink{A}
\end{align}$

Combining the $2$ equations and solving gives:

$\qquad$ $\begin{align}\pink{A} &= 75 \\\\
   \purple{B} &= 75\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Alma owns a lawn care business and recently performed some research on the size of $50$ lawns that she takes care of. 

Alma recalls that she is expected to take care of a total of $21$ acres of lawn for every $6$ customers that she serves. She also knows that she has $4$ customers with $1$ acre of lawn, $6$ customers with $2$ acres of lawn, and $9$ customers with $5$ acres of lawn.

Unfortunately, Alma can’t find the information on the number of customers that have $3$ or $4$ acre lawns. 

**Find the $2$ missing numbers from the data table below.**
 
Acres of lawn| Customers
:-: | :-: 
$1$ | $4$
$2$ | $6$ 
$3$ | [[☃ input-number 1]]
$4$ | [[☃ input-number 2]]
$5$ | $9$
Total | $50$
hints: Since Alma is expected to take care of $21$ acres of lawn for every $6$ customers, the expected number of acres of lawn per customer is $\dfrac{21}{6} = 3.5$.
Expected value is calculated by finding the value of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together. We need to find the relative frequencies.

Acres of lawn| Customers| Relative frequency | Relative frequency $\cdot$ acres of lawn
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$1$ | $4$|$\dfrac{4}{\red{50}} = 0.08$| $0.08$
$2$ | $6$|$\dfrac{6}{\red{50}} = 0.12$| $0.24$
$3$ | $\pink{A}$ | $\dfrac{\pink{A}}{\red{50}}$  | $\dfrac{3\pink{A}}{\red{50}}$
$4$ | $\purple{B}$| $\dfrac{\purple{B}}{\red{50}}$| $\dfrac{4\purple{B}}{\red{50}}$
$5$ | $9$|$\dfrac{9}{\red{50}} = 0.18$| $0.9$
Total | $\red{50}$| $1.00$| $3.5$


Note that the sum of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ acres of lawn}$ is expected value, which we know to be $3.5$.

We can now create an equation based on expected value:

$\qquad$ $\begin{align} 0.08 + 0.24 + \dfrac{3\pink{A}}{\red{50}}  + \dfrac{4\purple{B}}{\red{50}} + 0.9 &= 3.5 \\\\\\
3\pink{A} + 4\purple{B} &= 114  \end{align}$

We can also create an equation based on absolute frequencies:

$\qquad$ $\begin{align} 4 + 6 + \pink{A} + \purple{B} + 9 &= \red{50} \\\\\\
\purple{B} &= 31 - \pink{A}
\end{align}$

Combining the $2$ equations and solving gives:

$\qquad$ $\begin{align}\pink{A} &= 10 \\\\
   \purple{B} &= 21\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Divya spends all of her free time playing with her building blocks. She owns building block sets with $3$ pieces, $5$ pieces, and $10$ pieces.  

Divya knows she owns $5$ sets with $5$ pieces and $2$ sets with $10$ pieces.  She also knows that she averages $5$ pieces per set. This information is summarized in the table below.

**Fill in the missing blank with how many sets with $3$ pieces Divya owns.**

Pieces in a set| Sets
:-: | :-: 
$3$ | [[☃ input-number 1]]
$5$ | $5$ 
$10$ | $2$
hints:
Average is the same as expected value which is calculated by finding $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$ $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Sets}}{\text{Total sets}}$

Let $x$ be the number of $3$ piece sets that Divya owns.

Divya has a total of  $x + 5 + 2 = \red{x + 7}$ sets.

Pieces in a set| Sets | Relative frequency | Relative frequency $\cdot$ pieces in a set
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$3$ | $x$ | $\dfrac{x}{\red{x + 7}}$  | $\dfrac{3x}{\red{x + 7}}$
$5$ | $5$|$\dfrac{5}{\red{x + 7}}$ | $\dfrac{25}{\red{x + 7}}$
$10$ | $2$ | $\dfrac{2}{\red{x + 7}}$ | $\dfrac{20}{\red{x + 7}}$
Total | $\red{x + 7}$ | $1.00$| $5$

Note that the sum of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ pieces in a set}$ is expected value, which we know to be $5$.

$\qquad$ $\begin{align} 5
&= \dfrac{3x}{\red{x+7}} + \dfrac{25}{\red{x+7}} + \dfrac{20}{\red{x+7}} \\ \\ \\\\
5 &= \dfrac{3x+45}{\red{x+7}} \end{align}$

The solution of the equation is $x = \blue{5}$.

Pieces in a set| Sets
:-: | :-: 
$3$ | $\blue{5}$
$5$ | $5$ 
$10$ | $2$

assessment_items.question_content:
Pedro is an avid hunter and wants to better track his results.  He has had $4$ hunting trips where he has gone home with $4$ birds and $7$ hunting trips where he has gone home with $6$ birds. He also knows that he averages taking home $6.95$ birds per hunting trip. This information is summarized in the table below.

**Fill in the missing blank with how many hunting trips Pedro took where he brought home $9$ birds.**

Birds taken home| Hunting trips
:-: | :-: 
$4$ | $4$
$6$ | $7$ 
$9$ | [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
Average is the same as expected value which is calculated by finding $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$ $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Hunting trips}}{\text{Total hunting trips}}$

Let $x$ be the amount of hunting trips where Pedro took home $9$ birds.

Pedro went on a total of $4 + 7 + x = \red{x + 11}$ hunting trips. 

Birds taken home| Hunting trips | Relative frequency | Relative frequency $\cdot$ birds taken home
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$4$ | $4$ | $\dfrac{4}{\red{x + 11}}$  | $\dfrac{16}{\red{x + 11}}$
$6$ | $7$|$\dfrac{7}{\red{x + 11}}$ | $\dfrac{42}{\red{x + 11}}$
$9$ | $x$ | $\dfrac{x}{\red{x + 11}}$ | $\dfrac{9x}{\red{x + 11}}$
Total | $\red{x + 11}$ | $1.00$| $6.95$


Note that the sum of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ birds taken home}$  is expected value, which we know to be $6.95$.

$\qquad$ $\begin{align} 6.95
&= \dfrac{16}{\red{x+11}} + \dfrac{42}{\red{x+11}} + \dfrac{9x}{\red{x+11}} \\ \\ \\\\
6.95 &=\dfrac{9x+58}{\red{x+11}} \end{align}$

The solution of the equation is $x = \blue{9}$.

Birds taken home| Hunting trips
:-: | :-: 
$4$ | $4$
$6$ | $7$ 
$9$ | $\blue{9}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Kiran is recalling Easter egg hunts from her childhood.  She remembers that there were $2$ Easter egg hunts where she found $8$ eggs each and $4$ Easter egg hunts where she found $11$ eggs each.  She also knows that she averaged $11.6$ eggs per Easter egg hunt. This information is summarized in the table below.

**Fill in the missing blank with the number of hunts where Kiran found $14$ eggs.**

Eggs found| Easter egg hunts
:-: | :-: 
$8$ | $2$
$11$ | $4$ 
$14$ | [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
Average is the same as expected value which is calculated by finding $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$ $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{ Easter egg hunts}}{\text{Total Easter egg hunts}}$

Let $x$ be the amount of Easter egg hunts where Kiran found $14$ eggs.

Kiran has gone on a total of $2 + 4 + x = \red{x + 6}$ Easter egg hunts.

Eggs found| Easter Egg hunts | Relative frequency | Relative frequency $\cdot$ eggs found
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$8$ | $2$ | $\dfrac{2}{\red{x + 6}}$  | $\dfrac{16}{\red{x + 6}}$
$11$ | $4$|$\dfrac{4}{\red{x + 6}}$ | $\dfrac{44}{\red{x + 6}}$
$14$ | $x$ | $\dfrac{x}{\red{x + 6}}$ | $\dfrac{14x}{\red{x + 6}}$
Total | $\red{x + 6}$ | $1.00$| $11.6$


Note that the sum of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ eggs found}$  is expected value, which we know to be $11.6$.

$\qquad$ $\begin{align} 11.6
&= \dfrac{16}{\red{x+6}} + \dfrac{44}{\red{x+6}} + \dfrac{14x}{\red{x+6}} \\ \\ \\\\
11.6 &=\dfrac{14x +60}{\red{x+6}} \end{align}$

The solution of the equation is $x = \blue{4}$.

Eggs found| Easter egg hunts
:-: | :-: 
$8$ | $2$
$11$ | $4$ 
$14$ | $\blue{4}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Andrey just finished playing a word creation game with Roberto.  In this game, players create words and then are awarded points based on the difficulty of the word.  

Andrey knows that she created $4$ words worth $4$ points and $4$ words that were worth $8$ points. She also knows that she averaged $7$ points per word. This information is summarized in the table below.

**Fill in the missing blank with how many words Andrey created that were worth $11$ points.**

Points per word| Words
:-: | :-: 
$4$ | $4$
$8$ | $4$ 
$11$ | [[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
Average is the same as expected value which is calculated by finding $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$ $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Words}}{\text{Total words}}$

Let $x$ be the amount of words worth $11$ points that Andrey created. 

Andrey created $4 + 4+ x = \red{x + 8}$ total words.

Points per word| Words | Relative frequency | Relative frequency $\cdot$ points per word
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$4$ | $4$ | $\dfrac{4}{\red{x + 8}}$  | $\dfrac{16}{\red{x + 8}}$
$8$ | $4$|$\dfrac{4}{\red{x + 8}}$ | $\dfrac{32}{\red{x + 8}}$
$11$ | $x$ | $\dfrac{x}{\red{x + 8}}$ | $\dfrac{11x}{\red{x + 8}}$
Total | $\red{x + 8}$ | $1.00$| $7$


Note that the sum of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ points per word}$  is expected value, which we know to be $7$.

$\qquad$ $\begin{align} 7
&= \dfrac{16}{\red{x+8}} + \dfrac{32}{\red{x+8}} + \dfrac{11x}{\red{x+8}} \\ \\ \\\\
7 &=\dfrac{11x+48}{\red{x+8}} \end{align}$

The solution of the equation is $x = \blue{2}$.

Points per word| Words
:-: | :-: 
$4$ | $4$
$8$ | $4$ 
$11$ | $\blue{2}$

assessment_items.question_content:
John is looking back on his basketball season. He knows that he scored $100$ baskets worth $2$ points and $30$ baskets worth $3$ points this year.

He also knows that his scoring average for every basket he scored was $1.8$ points per basket. This information is summarized in the table below.  

**Fill in the missing blank with how many $1$-point baskets John scored this year.**

Point value| Baskets
:-: | :-: 
$1$ | [[☃ input-number 1]]
$2$ | $100$ 
$3$ | $30$
hints:
Scoring average is the same as expected value which is calculated by finding $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ value}$ for all possible values, and then adding them all together.

$\qquad$ $\text{Relative frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Baskets}}{\text{Total baskets}}$

Let $x$ be the amount of $1$-point baskets that John scored this year.

John scored $x + 100 + 30 = \red{x + 130}$ total baskets.

Point value| Baskets | Relative frequency | Relative frequency $\cdot$ point value
:-: | :-: | :-: | :-:
$1$ | $x$ | $\dfrac{x}{\red{x + 130}}$  | $\dfrac{x}{\red{x + 130}}$
$2$ | $100$|$\dfrac{100}{\red{x + 130}}$ | $\dfrac{200}{\red{x + 130}}$
$3$ | $30$ | $\dfrac{30}{\red{x + 130}}$ | $\dfrac{90}{\red{x + 130}}$
Total | $\red{x + 130}$ | $1.00$| $1.8$


Note that the sum of $\text{relative frequency $\cdot$ point value}$  is expected value, which we know to be $1.8$.

$\qquad$ $\begin{align} 1.8
&= \dfrac{x}{\red{x+130}} + \dfrac{200}{\red{x+130}} + \dfrac{90}{\red{x+130}} \\ \\ \\\\
1.8 &=\dfrac{x+290}{\red{x+130}} \end{align}$

The solution of the equation is $x = \blue{70}$.

Point value| Baskets
:-: | :-: 
$1$ | $\blue{70}$
$2$ | $100$ 
$3$ | $30$
